reasoning: Sepsis is a broad syndrome triggered by a systemic inflammatory response to many different types of infectious agents (bacteria, viruses, fungi), rather than a specific disease caused by a single pathogen. A universal 'sepsis vaccine' does not exist because standard vaccines provide active immunity against specific infectious agents. Current preventative options are vaccines targeted at specific pathogens (e.g., pneumococcal vaccine) rather than sepsis itself. The Wikipedia page 'List of infectious diseases' accurately reflects this by listing 'No' for the Sepsis vaccine status. Because vaccine development takes years to decades, and a single sepsis vaccine is fundamentally inconsistent with standard vaccine mechanisms, it is essentially guaranteed that the status will remain 'No'. Aside from potential transient Wikipedia vandalism, there is no reason to expect this value to change to 'Yes'.
